title: Fix it or replace it?
summary: On Edinburgh scaffolding, a stonemason and an architecture student argue gently about whether old walls should be repaired or torn down.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Mr Murray, my professor said this wall is too old. He said it is cheaper to take it down and build a new one.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Your professor is a clever man. He is also wrong.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Cheaper this year, yes. But this wall is from 1734. A new wall will last fifty years. This wall has already lasted three hundred.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: But repair takes a long time. Look at us. We have been here three weeks.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Yes. And we will be here three more. Six weeks. To save a wall that has stood for three hundred years. Is that a bad trade?

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: It is not just about the math. People need new buildings. Edinburgh needs houses.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Edinburgh needs houses. I do not say no. But Edinburgh also is its walls. If you take down every old wall, you have houses. You do not have Edinburgh.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Touch this stone. Feel the cut. A man cut it by hand. He is long dead. But his hand is still here. A new wall does not have that.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: My professor would say that is sentimental.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: Maybe. But sentimental is not a bad word. It just means you remember people.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: He would also say the cost is too high.

body.speaker: Mr Murray
body.text: The cost is high if you count only money. The cost of replacing is also high. You just do not see it on the bill.

annotations.meaningVi: phá nó xuống (phrasal verb: take down)
sự đánh đổi (giá trị này lấy giá trị khác)
đẽo nó bằng tay (chứ không bằng máy)
tình cảm / hoài niệm (đôi khi mang nghĩa hơi ủy mị)
chi phí / cái giá phải trả

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'for' and 'already'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nhấn việc một thứ đã kéo dài đến giờ. 'This wall has already lasted three hundred [years].' 'We have been here three weeks.' 'A wall that has stood for three hundred years.' 'Already' đặt giữa have/has và động từ phân từ; 'for + khoảng thời gian' nói tổng độ dài.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses present perfect to stress that something has continued up to now. 'This wall has already lasted three hundred [years].' 'We have been here three weeks.' 'A wall that has stood for three hundred years.' 'Already' goes between have/has and the past participle; 'for + duration' gives the total length.

translationVi:
Chloe: Bác Murray, giáo sư cháu bảo bức tường này cũ quá. Ông ấy nói phá đi xây mới rẻ hơn.
Mr Murray: Giáo sư của cháu là người sáng suốt. Cũng là người sai.
Chloe: Tại sao ạ?
Mr Murray: Năm nay thì rẻ hơn, đúng. Nhưng bức tường này từ năm 1734. Tường mới sẽ tồn tại năm mươi năm. Tường này đã tồn tại ba trăm năm rồi.
Chloe: Nhưng sửa thì lâu lắm. Nhìn chúng ta xem. Chúng ta đã ở đây ba tuần.
Mr Murray: Đúng. Và sẽ ở đây ba tuần nữa. Sáu tuần. Để cứu một bức tường đã đứng ba trăm năm. Có phải đổi tệ không?
Chloe: Không chỉ là chuyện toán. Người ta cần nhà mới. Edinburgh cần nhà.
Mr Murray: Edinburgh cần nhà. Bác không nói không. Nhưng Edinburgh cũng chính là những bức tường của nó. Nếu cháu phá hết tường cũ, cháu có nhà. Cháu không còn Edinburgh.
Chloe: Khác nhau ở đâu, thực ra ạ?
Mr Murray: Cháu sờ viên đá này đi. Cảm nhận đường cắt. Một người đã đẽo nó bằng tay. Ông ta mất lâu rồi. Nhưng bàn tay ông ấy vẫn còn đây. Tường mới không có cái đó.
Chloe: Giáo sư cháu sẽ nói đó là sự ủy mị.
Mr Murray: Có thể. Nhưng 'ủy mị' không phải từ xấu. Nó chỉ có nghĩa là cháu nhớ đến con người.
Chloe: Ông ấy cũng sẽ nói chi phí quá cao.
Mr Murray: Chi phí cao nếu cháu chỉ tính tiền. Chi phí thay thế cũng cao. Chỉ là cháu không thấy nó trên hóa đơn.

questions.prompt: How long will a new wall last, according to Mr Murray?
questions.explanation: 'A new wall will last fifty years.'

questions.prompt: How long has the old wall lasted?
questions.explanation: 'This wall has already lasted three hundred.'

questions.prompt: How long has the repair work already taken?
What does Mr Murray mean by 'You do not have Edinburgh'?
options: If every old wall is torn down, the city loses what makes it itself
Edinburgh will be empty of people
There will be no more streets
questions.hint: Look at his line about Edinburgh.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Murray ask Chloe to do with the stone?
options: Touch it and feel the hand-cut surface

questions.prompt: How does Mr Murray reply to 'sentimental'?
options: It is not a bad word — it just means you remember people
He has never heard the word
He thinks it is a Scottish word

questions.prompt: What does Mr Murray say about cost?
options: The cost of replacing is also high — you just do not see it on the bill
Cost does not matter to him
Repair is always cheaper than replacement

questions.prompt: Does Mr Murray oppose new houses?
options: No — he says Edinburgh needs houses; he just resists losing all the old walls
Yes — he opposes any new construction
questions.explanation: 'Edinburgh needs houses. I do not say no.'

options: Repair has invisible value that pure cost calculations miss — keeping old walls keeps something human
questions.explanation: Mr Murray makes exactly this argument.
questions.hint: Think about both his arguments together.

options: Put a new thing in place of an old one

cloze.template:
Chloe: Mr Murray, my professor said this {{b1}} is too old. He said it is cheaper to take it down and build a new one.
Mr Murray: Your professor is a clever man. He is also wrong.
Chloe: Why?
Mr Murray: Cheaper this year, yes. But this wall is from {{b2}}. A new wall will last fifty years. This wall has already lasted three hundred.
Chloe: But repair takes a long time. Look at us. We have been here three {{b3}}.
Mr Murray: Yes. And we will be here three more. Six weeks. To save a wall that has stood for three {{b4}} years. Is that a bad trade?
Chloe: It is not just about the math. People need new buildings. Edinburgh needs houses.
Mr Murray: Edinburgh needs houses. I do not say no. But Edinburgh also is its walls. If you take down every old wall, you have houses. You do not have {{b5}}.
Chloe: What is the difference, really?
Mr Murray: Touch this stone. Feel the cut. A man cut it by {{b6}}. He is long dead. But his hand is still here. A new wall does not have that.
Chloe: My professor would say that is {{b7}}.
Mr Murray: Maybe. But sentimental is not a bad word. It just means you {{b8}} people.
Chloe: He would also say the cost is too high.
Mr Murray: The cost is high if you count only {{b9}}. The cost of replacing is also high. You just do not see it on the {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Three weeks of repair so far.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Murray says of replacement, 'You just do not see it on the bill.' Think of one decision your city, school, or family made recently where the official cost was low but something unseen was lost. Was the trade fair? Who decided?
